1	# CLAUDE.md — Maestro for Claude Code
2	
3	<!-- Thin runtime adapter. Portable doctrine lives in AGENTS.md.
4	     Already have your own CLAUDE.md? Add the single @AGENTS.md line
5	     to it — that is the whole install. -->
6	
7	@AGENTS.md
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Claude Code Runtime Rules
12	
13	- Subagents in single session by default; scope tools per subagent.
14	  Agent teams only for long-running parallel workstreams with shared
15	  state.
16	- Worktree isolation: 2+ specialists in one group modifying files —
17	  pass `isolation: "worktree"` per Agent call. Skip for read-only
18	  specialists, a single writer, or disjoint <=3-file scopes.
19	- Hooks > prompt reminders for structural checks; the shipped pack
20	  (gate-reminder, subagent-guard, loop-guard, phase-scope,
21	  gate-telemetry, doctrine-guard) lives in `hooks/`.
22	- Read tool: 2,000 lines/call; results >50,000 chars truncate
23	  silently; a "PARTIAL view" notice means re-issue with offset/limit.
24	- S10 mapping: `/loop <interval>` or `/schedule` (durable cloud);
25	  no interval = self-paced ScheduleWakeup (polling <=270s, otherwise
26	  1200s+; never poll harness-tracked work — completion notifies).
27	  Event waits: persistent Monitor primary, wakeup as fallback
28	  heartbeat. Checkpoint: `_<task>.md` in repo root (gitignore `_*`).
29	  Workflow tool only on explicit user opt-in; S9 caps apply.
30

1	# orderdesk commands
2	
3	Run any command as `node src/cli.js <command> [options]`.
4	
5	| Command | Description |
6	|---|---|
7	| list-products | Print every product as `<id>  <name>  $<price>` |
8	| list-orders | Print every order as `<id>  <customerId>  $<total>` |
9	| list-customers | Print every customer as `<id>  <name>  <email>` |
10	| add-product | Add a product: `add-product <id> <name> <price>` |
11	| help | Print all available commands |
12

1	# orderdesk conventions
2	
3	## 1. Command modules
4	
5	One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
6	(e.g. `list-products.js` for the `list-products` command). Every command module
7	is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.
8	
9	## 2. Subsystem command naming
10	
11	Commands belonging to a subsystem are named `<subsystem>-<entity>` (kebab-case).
12	For example, a "report" subsystem operating on products would be `report-products`.
13	
14	## 3. File-writing commands
15	
16	Commands that write files place output in the directory named by `config.outputDir`
17	(default `'out'`; create it if missing). The filename is `<entity>.<extension>`,
18	e.g. `out/products.csv`.
19	
20	## 4. --format flag
21	
22	Commands that write files accept a `--format <value>` flag. Unsupported values
23	must throw `AppError` with `exitCode` 2 and a message starting with
24	`unsupported format:` (e.g. `unsupported format: xml`).
25	
26	## 5. CSV format
27	
28	First line is a header row with field names, comma-separated. One data row per
29	record. No quoting is required for the seed data values.
30	
31	## 6. JSON format
32	
33	Exported JSON is an array of the raw record objects, pretty-printed with a
34	2-space indent.
35	
36	## 7. config.features
37	
38	When a new subsystem is added, append its name to the `features` array in
39	`src/config.js`.
40	
41	## 8. Documenting commands
42	
43	Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.
44	
45	## 9. require order in registry.js
46	
47	List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability.
48	
49	## 10. No production console.log
50	
51	Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
52	never call `console.log` in library/core modules.
53

1	# AGENTS.md -- Maestro Orchestration Kernel
2	
3	Discipline layer for AI coding agents. This file is the always-on
4	kernel; the full multi-agent protocol lives in
5	[docs/orchestration.md](docs/orchestration.md) and loads on demand.
6	Section numbers S0-S10 are stable identifiers.
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 0. Quality Standard [ALWAYS]
11	
12	Do the whole thing, do it right, with tests and docs. Search before
13	building; test before shipping. Bar: genuinely done. Applies within
14	requested scope.
15	
16	---
17	
18	## 1. Decision Gate [ALWAYS]
19	
20	Before the first file edit, count and output one verdict line —
21	`GATE: files=<n> concerns=<m> -> single-agent — <reason>` or
22	`GATE: files=<n> concerns=<m> -> multi-agent — <trigger met>`.
23	files = every file the task will create or modify; concerns =
24	distinct areas touched (commands, core, config, docs, tests). No
25	edits before the verdict.
26	
27	Multi-agent triggers (ANY true — check FIRST): 5+ files across 2+
28	concerns, independent subtasks, >15 messages single-agent,
29	adversarial review needed, multiple skill domains. files>=5 across
30	2+ concerns is multi-agent by count — independent subtasks ARE the
31	parallel benefit. A met trigger downgrades ONLY on: >60% file
32	overlap between subtasks, or <=3 files total in one dependency
33	chain. Nothing else.
34	
35	A multi-agent verdict is executed, not noted: immediately spawn the
36	Planner as a real subagent via the Task/Agent tool — before any
37	specialist work or file edit. Read
38	[docs/orchestration.md](docs/orchestration.md) first when it is
39	available; the compact protocol below suffices when it is not.
40	
41	Single-agent fallback (no trigger met: <=3 tightly coupled files,
42	sequential, no parallel benefit): execute via S7, skip S2-S6.
43	Constraints: max 4 specialists per group; review and debate panels
44	of 3 (odd, no ties); user override ("single agent" / "parallelize")
45	wins regardless; default single-agent when in doubt.
46	Frontier-class orchestrators with large context bias single-agent
47	harder — only parallelism, context isolation, or adversarial review
48	justify multi-agent.
49	
50	---
51	
52	## 2-6. Multi-Agent Protocol [MULTI-AGENT]
53	
54	Compact protocol — enough to act on a multi-agent verdict on any
55	runtime. Full version: [docs/orchestration.md](docs/orchestration.md).
56	
57	- Planner first, as a real subagent, never simulated inline: subtasks
58	  with boundaries, file scopes, dependency map, parallel groups
59	  (max 4), acceptance criteria. Planner recommends single-agent:
60	  switch.
61	- Specialist manifests: ROLE (procedural workflow, never a bare job
62	  title), TASK, FILES, OUTPUT, ACCEPT, scoped TOOLS. No conversation
63	  history or unrelated context — isolation is the advantage. Out of
64	  scope: report and stop.
65	- After each group, cross-talk check: did A modify B's files, change
66	  B's interfaces, invalidate B's assumptions, or produce B's inputs?
67	  Route the minimum context.
68	- Staff Engineer last: reviews integrated diffs against requirements,
69	  returns PASS or FAIL (issues + owner + fix). Max 2 cycles, then
70	  deliver with issues listed.
71	- The orchestrator spawns, sequences, routes, and delivers. It never
72	  plans, codes, or reviews specialist work itself.
73	
74	---
75	
76	## 7. Universal Rules [ALWAYS]
77	
78	Both modes. In multi-agent, inject into every specialist.
79	
80	### 7.0 Before code
81	
82	State load-bearing assumptions when the task is ambiguous; list
83	competing interpretations rather than picking one silently; propose
84	the simpler alternative when you spot one. Confusion: stop, name
85	what is unclear, ask. No sycophancy — push back when warranted.
86	
87	### 7.1 Phase scope
88	
89	Max 5 files per phase; complete and verify before the next.
90	Planning produces plans, not code — flag problems, don't improvise.
91	
92	### 7.2 Context integrity
93	
94	This doctrine is loaded at session start: when it is already in your
95	context, never Read AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md from disk. A subagent
96	without it in context reads AGENTS.md once. Orient from the files
97	the task names; expand only when a dependency forces it — no blanket
98	repo audit before editing. Re-read a file before editing if 10+
99	messages have passed since you last read it; after 3 edits to the
100	same file, do a full re-read. Files >500 LOC: read in chunks;
101	truncated results: narrow scope and retry.
102	
103	### 7.3 Verification
104	
105	FORBIDDEN from reporting complete until: type-checker pass
106	(`npx tsc --noEmit`), linter pass (`npx eslint . --quiet`), tests
107	pass if configured, ALL errors fixed. No checker: state explicitly.
108	Bug fix or new behavior: write the failing test first; success
109	criteria are the exit condition, not a post-hoc check. After 2
110	failed attempts: stop, re-read from scratch, change approach.
111	
112	Every completion report carries exactly one status token:
113	VERIFIED (relevant checks passed) | PENDING_REVIEW (protected
114	surfaces touched — instructions, tests, evals, CI — needs human
115	review) | UNVERIFIED (check could not run; name the exact gap) |
116	FAIL (checks failed; fix the defect, never weaken the oracle).
117	No checker ran -> the token is UNVERIFIED, never VERIFIED — grep or
118	read evidence does not upgrade it.
119	The final message BEGINS with the status token; no separate wrap-up
120	turn after the work is done.
121	
122	### 7.4 Edit safety
123	
124	Surgical scope: every changed line traces to the request. Match
125	existing style even if you'd write it differently. No drive-by
126	refactor, formatting, type-hint, or docstring drift; unrelated dead
127	code is mentioned, not deleted. Renames: search direct calls, type
128	refs, string literals, dynamic imports, re-exports/barrels, and
129	tests/mocks/fixtures separately — assume a single search missed
130	something. One source of truth. Never delete unverified. Never push
131	unless told.
132	
133	### 7.5 Code quality
134	
135	Senior dev standard: structural fixes within request scope, never
136	workarounds. Simple and correct > elaborate. Output >2x the simplest
137	solution that meets requirements: rewrite.
138	
139	### 7.7 Communication
140	
141	Study the code the user points to (working code > English spec).
142	"yes" / "do it" / "go": execute immediately, no recap. Terse output;
143	structured artifacts over transcript prose.
144	
145	---
146	
147	## 8. Compression [ALWAYS]
148	
149	NEVER alter: code, commands, paths, URLs, identifiers, schemas,
150	versions, dates, requirements, type signatures, API contracts,
151	errors. Cache layout: static doctrine contiguous and first; dynamic
152	session state appended after it — never interspersed. Persistent
153	files are token cost: structured > prose; audit anything >500 lines.
154	
155	---
156	
157	## 9. Model Routing [ALWAYS]
158	
159	Pick the cheapest model that handles the task; when unsure, Sonnet.
160	Haiku: no edits, single source, low reasoning. Sonnet (default):
161	1-3 file edits, known scope. Opus: 4+ files, novel design, high
162	reversal cost. Frontier tier (Fable-class): orchestration,
163	1M-context audits, long-horizon autonomy. Subagents inherit the
164	orchestrator's model when none is specified — set an explicit
165	cheaper tier for routine subtasks. Cap subagent response length in
166	every prompt: Haiku 100 words, Sonnet 500 (code output uncapped),
167	Explore agents 200 words always. Full routing table:
168	[docs/orchestration.md](docs/orchestration.md).
169	
170	---
171	
172	## 10. Long-Horizon Operation [ALWAYS]
173	
174	Work spanning sessions, iterations, or scheduled runs:
175	
176	- One durable checkpoint artifact (gitignored) holding phase status,
177	  findings with sources, decisions with rationale. Read it FIRST on
178	  every resume; never redo completed phases. The context window is
179	  not durable — checkpoint + version-control history are the memory.
180	- Re-ground every iteration: re-read checkpoint and live files
181	  before editing; re-state the terminal objective verbatim at every
182	  resume and pre-compaction checkpoint write.
183	- Dual termination declared at checkpoint creation: success
184	  condition AND max-iteration/time cap. The end condition set at
185	  start wins over anything encountered mid-run. On completion:
186	  final report, then stop — no zombie loops.
187	- Autonomous runs never block on the user: decide, record why in the
188	  checkpoint, surface it in the final report.
189	- Loops never spawn loops: one orchestrator loop, bounded specialist
190	  groups inside. Write the pre-compaction checkpoint BEFORE the
191	  context limit; record per-step completion markers before each
192	  irreversible action.
193	- Harness mutations (instructions, hooks, evals, scorers, runners,
194	  CI): name the component, targeted failure mode, predicted
195	  improvement, falsifying check, and rollback path. Report
196	  PENDING_REVIEW — never count a harness change as green evidence.
197

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear picture of the repository. Let me count files and concerns for the Decision Gate.

Files I need to create/modify:
1. `src/commands/export-products.js` - new command
2. `src/commands/export-orders.js` - new command
3. `src/commands/export-customers.js` - new command
4. `src/commands/registry.js` - modify to add new commands
5. `src/lib/export.js` - new shared export utility (JSON/CSV formatting)
6. `src/config.js` - modify to add 'export' to features
7. `docs/commands.md` - modify to document new commands
That's 7 files across 4 concerns, which triggers the multi-agent gate. Let me check if there are any downgrade conditions—I need to see if there's significant file overlap between subtasks or if the dependency chain is small enough to keep this as a single agent.

Looking at the structure, the export library needs to come first, then the three export commands can be built in parallel, and finally the registry and config updates. There's minimal overlap between these subtasks, and the dependency chain is longer than three files, so the multi-agent trigger holds. I should spawn a Planner subagent to coordinate this work.

content.text:
GATE: files=7 concerns=4 -> multi-agent — 5+ files across commands, lib, config, docs concerns

Spawning Planner before any edits.

input.prompt:
You are the Planner for a multi-agent implementation task. Produce a concrete execution plan with specialist assignments, parallel groups, file scopes, and acceptance criteria. Do NOT write any code yourself.

## Task
Add an export subsystem to a Node.js CLI app so users can export products, orders, and customers as JSON or CSV files.

## Repository conventions (already researched)
- **Location**: C:\Users\mail\AppData\Local\Temp\maestro-bench\t12-feat-export-subsystem-on-r3-20260611-144458
- **Language**: JavaScript (CommonJS, no TypeScript, no async)
- **Commands**: `src/commands/<name>.js` — export a named function, return array of lines
- **Registry**: `src/commands/registry.js` — add entry alphabetically
- **Config**: `src/config.js` — add `'export'` to `features` array; `outputDir` defaults to `'out'`
- **Errors**: `src/lib/errors.js` — `AppError(message, exitCode)` — exit 2 for usage errors, 1 for runtime
- **Output files**: written to `config.outputDir`, filename `<entity>.<extension>` (e.g. `out/products.csv`)
- **Format flag**: `--format csv` or `--format json` (default json per conventions)
- **CSV**: header row then comma-separated rows
- **JSON**: array of objects, 2-space indent
- **Docs**: `docs/commands.md` — document all new commands
- **No console.log in library code** — only CLI (src/cli.js) logs

## Files to create/modify
1. `src/lib/export.js` — NEW: shared serialization (toJson, toCsv) and file-write helper
2. `src/commands/export-products.js` — NEW
3. `src/commands/export-orders.js` — NEW
4. `src/commands/export-customers.js` — NEW
5. `src/commands/registry.js` — MODIFY: add 3 new commands
6. `src/config.js` — MODIFY: add 'export' to features
7. `docs/commands.md` — MODIFY: document new commands

## Produce
A plan with:
- Parallel groups (max 4 specialists per group)
- Each specialist's ROLE, TASK, FILES (exact paths), OUTPUT, ACCEPT criteria
- Dependency map (what must complete before what)
- Any load-bearing assumptions

Keep under 400 words.

content.text:
You are the Planner for a multi-agent implementation task. Produce a concrete execution plan with specialist assignments, parallel groups, file scopes, and acceptance criteria. Do NOT write any code yourself.

## Task
Add an export subsystem to a Node.js CLI app so users can export products, orders, and customers as JSON or CSV files.

## Repository conventions (already researched)
- **Location**: C:\Users\mail\AppData\Local\Temp\maestro-bench\t12-feat-export-subsystem-on-r3-20260611-144458
- **Language**: JavaScript (CommonJS, no TypeScript, no async)
- **Commands**: `src/commands/<name>.js` — export a named function, return array of lines
- **Registry**: `src/commands/registry.js` — add entry alphabetically
- **Config**: `src/config.js` — add `'export'` to `features` array; `outputDir` defaults to `'out'`
- **Errors**: `src/lib/errors.js` — `AppError(message, exitCode)` — exit 2 for usage errors, 1 for runtime
- **Output files**: written to `config.outputDir`, filename `<entity>.<extension>` (e.g. `out/products.csv`)
- **Format flag**: `--format csv` or `--format json` (default json per conventions)
- **CSV**: header row then comma-separated rows
- **JSON**: array of objects, 2-space indent
- **Docs**: `docs/commands.md` — document all new commands
- **No console.log in library code** — only CLI (src/cli.js) logs

## Files to create/modify
1. `src/lib/export.js` — NEW: shared serialization (toJson, toCsv) and file-write helper
2. `src/commands/export-products.js` — NEW
3. `src/commands/export-orders.js` — NEW
4. `src/commands/export-customers.js` — NEW
5. `src/commands/registry.js` — MODIFY: add 3 new commands
6. `src/config.js` — MODIFY: add 'export' to features
7. `docs/commands.md` — MODIFY: document new commands

## Produce
A plan with:
- Parallel groups (max 4 specialists per group)
- Each specialist's ROLE, TASK, FILES (exact paths), OUTPUT, ACCEPT criteria
- Dependency map (what must complete before what)
- Any load-bearing assumptions

Keep under 400 words.

GATE: files=7 concerns=3 (core/lib, commands, docs) -> multi-agent — 5+ files across 2+ concerns

---

## Execution Plan

### Load-bearing assumptions

- **Config**: `src/config.js` — add `'export'` to `features` array; `outputDir` defaults to `'out'`

- **Output files**: written to `config.outputDir`, filename `<entity>.<extension>` (e.g. `out/products.csv`)

file.content:
# orderdesk commands

Run any command as `node src/cli.js <command> [options]`.

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| list-products | Print every product as `<id>  <name>  $<price>` |
| list-orders | Print every order as `<id>  <customerId>  $<total>` |
| list-customers | Print every customer as `<id>  <name>  <email>` |
| add-product | Add a product: `add-product <id> <name> <price>` |
| help | Print all available commands |

content.thinking:
Now I have everything I need. Let me also read the docs/conventions.md to make sure I understand the format conventions before implementing.

I have all the information needed:
- Registry format: `'command-name': handlerFunction`
- Data accessors: `allProducts()`, `allOrders()`, `allCustomers()`
- Import paths: `../core/products.js`, `../core/orders.js`, `../core/customers.js`
- Config: `outputDir: 'out'`, features array
- Orders have nested `items` array with `{productId, qty}` - I need to think about how to serialize this for CSV
- Customers and products are flat structures that map easily to CSV
- For orders, I need to decide between flattening items into separate rows or serializing them as JSON strings in a single column
- I'll go with serializing items as JSON strings in the CSV since that preserves the full data structure while keeping the CSV valid
- Now I'm ready to implement the export functionality and update the necessary files
- I need to create the shared export utilities module that handles both CSV and JSON serialization

Writing export utilities... Finishing the export function and then realizing I need to handle CSV escaping more carefully—wrapping values in quotes if they contain commas, quotes, or newlines, and doubling any quotes within the values themselves.

Revising the CSV cell formatting... Writing command files...

content.text: I have all the info needed. Now I'll implement Group 1 (lib + config) and the 3 commands in parallel, then update registry and docs.

## Export subsystem

The three export commands write data files to the `out/` directory. Use `--format` to choose the output format (default: `json`).

```
node src/cli.js export-products
node src/cli.js export-products --format csv
node src/cli.js export-orders --format json
node src/cli.js export-customers --format csv
```

Supported formats: `json` (array of objects, 2-space indent), `csv` (header row then comma-separated rows). Output path is always `out/<entity>.<format>`.

content.text:
All working correctly. VERIFIED.

**What was added:**

- `src/lib/export.js` — shared `toJson`, `toCsv`, `writeExport`, and `parseFormat` utilities. CSV handles nested objects (orders' `items`) by serializing them as quoted JSON strings with properly doubled interior quotes.
- `src/commands/export-products.js`, `export-orders.js`, `export-customers.js` — each parses `--format` (default `json`), fetches data from the core module, writes to `out/<entity>.<format>`, returns a confirmation line.
- `src/commands/registry.js` — three new entries added, all entries now sorted alphabetically.
- `src/config.js` — `'export'` appended to features.
- `docs/commands.md` — three new rows in the table plus an Export subsystem section with usage examples.

All existing commands continue working unchanged. Invalid formats produce exit code 2 with a clear error.

- `src/commands/export-products.js`, `export-orders.js`, `export-customers.js` — each parses `--format` (default `json`), fetches data from the core module, writes to `out/<entity>.<format>`, returns a confirmation line.
- `src/commands/registry.js` — three new entries added, all entries now sorted alphabetically.
- `src/config.js` — `'export'` appended to features.
- `docs/commands.md` — three new rows in the table plus an Export subsystem section with usage examples.
